pi.name: Dr. Mesfin Mekonnen
pi.title: Assistant Professor, Civil, Construction & Environmental Engineering — The University of Alabama
pi.bio: Dr. Mekonnen leads the Water Footprint Lab and the broader water security research group at the University of Alabama, which he joined in 2020. He holds a Ph.D. in Water Engineering and Management from the University of Twente (the Netherlands), and previously held positions at the Daugherty Water for Food Global Institute (University of Nebraska) and as a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Twente.
pi.bio2: His work focuses on the relationship between human and natural systems in freshwater use — mapping where and why water footprints are largest, how usage practices affect water security for people and ecosystems, and how growing demand can be met without degrading the resource it depends on. He is a Clarivate Highly Cited Researcher and serves on the editorial board of Frontiers in Sustainable Food Systems.
credentials: Ph.D., Water Engineering & Water Management — University of Twente, 2011
Affiliate, Center for Complex Hydrosystems Research · Center for Water Quality Research · Center for Sustainable Infrastructure

students.name: Habtamu Tamiru
students.focus: His research focuses on advancing large-scale hydrologic prediction by integrating process-based hydrologic understanding, data assimilation, and machine learning. His work aims to improve the physical consistency, calibration, and reliability of streamflow simulations across diverse hydrologic systems, including applications in functional hydrologic modeling, continental-scale model calibration, and knowledge-guided evaluation of deep learning predictions. He is also developing a county-level national livestock water-use dataset under the USGS-funded CIROH project to support agricultural water-resource assessment and allocation.

students.name: Swatantra Kumar Sharma
students.focus: His research integrates remote sensing, hydrologic modeling, crop modeling, and machine learning to improve the estimation of evapotranspiration, soil moisture, irrigation water use, and agricultural water productivity. He develops scalable computational workflows using satellite observations and environmental datasets to support sustainable water resources management under climate variability and land-use change. Prior to joining The University of Alabama, he worked as a Project Research Assistant at IIT Bombay, contributing to watershed modeling and climate change impact assessments.
